SBOM & Vulnerability Management with Dependency-Track

ℹ️  About this page
This page describes the SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) pipeline and how Dependency-Track is used to continuously monitor, triage, and manage software vulnerabilities across all projects. It covers SBOM generation, upload, vulnerability triage workflows, comments, suppression, and automated reporting.

Last updated: 23 April 2026

📋  Contents

  1. What is an SBOM?
  2. How SBOMs Are Generated
  3. Dependency-Track Overview
  4. Managing Vulnerabilities
  5. Analysis & Actions
  6. Reporting
  7. Pipeline Overview
  8. API Explorer & Integration Ecosystem
  9. Benefits Summary

1. What is an SBOM?

An SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) is a formal, machine-readable inventory of every open-source and third-party component included in an application — along with their versions, licences, and dependency relationships. It is the foundation for understanding what your software is made of and identifying where security risks exist.

What an SBOM records

  • Component name and version
  • Package supplier / origin
  • Dependency relationships (direct and transitive)
  • Licence identifiers (SPDX format)
  • Known vulnerability references (CVE cross-links)

CycloneDX Format

All SBOMs in this pipeline use the CycloneDX standard (an OWASP project). CycloneDX is natively supported by Dependency-Track, cdxgen, and the CycloneDX Maven / Gradle plugins. SBOMs are stored as JSON files.

💡  Why CycloneDX?
CycloneDX is the recommended SBOM format for Dependency-Track. SPDX format is also supported but CycloneDX provides richer metadata for vulnerability correlation.

Why do SBOMs matter?

  • Provides complete visibility into all transitive dependencies — not just direct ones
  • Enables rapid impact assessment when a new CVE is published
  • Supports licence compliance and open-source governance
  • Creates an auditable supply-chain record
  • Required by emerging software security regulations and standards

2. How SBOMs Are Generated

Two complementary approaches are used, both feeding directly into Dependency-Track.

2a. cdxgen — Any Language / Repository

cdxgen is a polyglot SBOM generator that scans source trees and lock files. It is used for repositories that do not use Maven as their build tool.

  • Supports: Java, Node.js, Python, .NET, Go, Ruby, and more
  • Scans source tree and dependency lock files automatically
  • Runs as a Jenkins pipeline step via the cdxgenRepo() shared library call
  • No changes are needed to the target repository
  • Supports pinned image versions for reproducible pipeline builds
💡  Java repositories
Use cdxgenRepo(env.SBOM_FILE, true, '', '17') for Java repos to enable the Java-specific cdxgen image which can run Maven to resolve the full dependency tree.

2b. CycloneDX Maven Plugin — In-House Java Code

For internally developed Java applications, the CycloneDX Maven plugin is added directly to pom.xml. The SBOM is generated and published to Dependency-Track automatically as part of the standard Maven build.

  • Zero additional tooling — everything runs inside the existing mvn package lifecycle
  • Generates a precise SBOM reflecting the exact resolved dependency tree
  • Vulnerability status is visible in Dependency-Track the moment the build lands
  • Policy violations surface in the CI pipeline — not weeks later
  • Developers get immediate feedback on new vulnerabilities introduced by their changes
Add to pom.xmlxml
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.cyclonedx</groupId>
  <artifactId>cyclonedx-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>2.9.1</version>
  <executions>
    <execution>
      <phase>package</phase>
      <goals>
        <goal>makeAggregateBom</goal>
      </goals>
    </execution>
  </executions>
  <configuration>
    <outputFormat>json</outputFormat>
    <outputName>sbom</outputName>
  </configuration>
</plugin>

Maven Pipeline Flow

  1. Developer pushes code to Git → Jenkins pipeline triggered
  2. CycloneDX Maven plugin runs during mvn package
  3. Plugin generates sbom.json reflecting the resolved dependency tree
  4. Jenkins uploadSBOM() shared library step posts the SBOM to Dependency-Track
  5. Dependency-Track enriches components with NVD / OSV / GitHub Advisory data
  6. Vulnerabilities are visible immediately in the Dependency-Track dashboard
📝  Automatic project management
The uploadSBOM() Jenkins shared library step handles project creation/lookup and parent-project grouping automatically. No manual Dependency-Track configuration is needed per project.

2c. Syft — Docker / Container Images

Syft (by Anchore) scans Docker image layers to generate a full container SBOM — capturing every OS package and language library baked into the image, not just the application dependencies declared in a manifest.

  • Scans a pulled or locally built Docker image
  • Identifies OS packages (Alpine, Debian, RHEL) and language libraries inside the image
  • Outputs CycloneDX JSON — uploads directly to Dependency-Track via uploadSBOM()
  • Used via the syftImage() Jenkins shared-library step
  • Often used alongside Grype (also by Anchore) for pre-upload vulnerability triage
💡  Syft + Grype Together
Run grypeScan() after syftImage() to get an immediate vulnerability report before the SBOM is uploaded. Grype uses the same intelligence sources as Dependency-Track and is fast enough to run on every build.

3. Dependency-Track Overview

Dependency-Track is an OWASP flagship platform that ingests CycloneDX SBOMs and continuously monitors every component for new vulnerabilities. It provides a central, always-current view of risk across the entire software portfolio.

FeatureDescription
Continuous MonitoringChecks every component against NVD, OSV, GitHub Advisory, and Sonatype OSS Index automatically — no manual CVE searches required.
Policy EngineDefine licence policies, severity thresholds, and component allow/block lists. Violations are surfaced instantly and can break the build.
Portfolio / Collection ViewGroup child projects into an aggregate collection project. See combined risk across a product portfolio at a glance.
REST APIFully headless via REST API. All pipeline integrations (SBOM upload, report export) use the API — no manual UI interaction needed.
Vulnerability TriageEach finding can be given an analysis state, a justification, analyst comments, and optionally suppressed — all recorded with a full audit trail.
PDF ReportingJenkins pipelines generate branded PDF reports (vulnerability and SBOM component) automatically and archive them as build artefacts.

Vulnerability Intelligence Sources

Dependency-Track queries the following feeds automatically on a rolling schedule. No manual CVE searching is required — new vulnerabilities are matched against your entire component inventory the moment a feed refreshes.

SourceMaintained byScopeNotes
NVD — National Vulnerability DatabaseNISTAll languages & ecosystemsCanonical CVE registry with CVSS v2/v3/v4 scores. Primary authoritative source.
OSV — Open Source VulnerabilitiesGooglePyPI, npm, Maven, Go, Cargo, Ruby gems & moreOpen database with precise package-URL matching; often earlier disclosure than NVD.
GitHub Advisory DatabaseGitHub / communityGitHub-hosted packages across all ecosystemsCurated advisories tied directly to package versions; highly accurate matching.
Sonatype OSS IndexSonatypeMaven Central, npmComponent intelligence with licence and ecosystem metadata; strong Java coverage.
VulnDBRisk Based SecurityAll ecosystemsOptional commercial feed. Broader coverage and earlier disclosure than NVD. Configurable in DT settings.

SBOM Generation & Detection Tools

The pipeline supports multiple tools for generating SBOMs and scanning for vulnerabilities — choose the right tool for the project type.

ToolTypeUse CaseJenkins Step
cdxgenSBOM GeneratorPolyglot — Java, Node.js, Python, Go, .NET, Ruby, Cargo & more. Source-tree and lock-file scanning.cdxgenRepo()
CycloneDX Maven PluginSBOM GeneratorJava/Maven — embedded in pom.xml; generates an exact resolved-dependency-tree SBOM at build time.Runs automatically via mvn package
Syft (Anchore)SBOM GeneratorDocker / container images — scans image layers to produce a full container SBOM.syftImage()
Grype (Anchore)Vulnerability ScannerConsumes a Syft or cdxgen SBOM and scans for known vulnerabilities. Used for pre-upload triage.grypeScan()
CycloneDX Gradle / Node / .NETSBOM GeneratorEcosystem-specific build plugins that mirror the Maven plugin approach for Gradle, npm, and .NET SDK projects.Language-native build steps
💡  Consistent Format Across All Tools
All tools produce CycloneDX JSON SBOMs that feed directly into Dependency-Track via the uploadSBOM() Jenkins shared-library step. The format is consistent regardless of the generator used.

4. Managing Vulnerabilities

Dependency-Track surfaces every finding. Your team then triages, analyses, and resolves each one — all recorded within the platform to provide a full audit trail.

StepActionNotes
1 Ingest SBOMSBOM uploaded via Jenkins pipeline.DT resolves all components and cross-references vulnerability feeds.
2 Review FindingsOpen the project in Dependency-Track.Findings are listed by severity: CRITICAL HIGH MEDIUM LOW
3 TriageAssess each finding.Decide: fix immediately, accept risk with justification, suppress, or escalate to engineering.
4 Record AnalysisDocument your decision inside Dependency-Track.Set the analysis state, add an analyst comment, optionally suppress. All actions are time-stamped and attributed.
5 Remediate & Re-scanEngineering upgrades the dependency.The next pipeline run re-uploads the SBOM. The finding disappears from the dashboard automatically.

5. Vulnerability Analysis — Comments, Suppression & State

Every finding in Dependency-Track can be analysed, commented on, and formally recorded. This creates an audit trail of all security decisions made by the team.

Analysis States

Analysis StateMeaningWhen to use
NOT_AFFECTEDThe vulnerability does not apply to this deployment context.The affected code path is unused, a mitigating control is in place, or the component is used in a way that is not exploitable.
IN_TRIAGEThe finding is being actively investigated.Interim state — use while the team is determining the impact and deciding on the appropriate response.
EXPLOITABLEConfirmed — the vulnerability is present and exploitable.Immediate remediation required. Escalate to engineering.
RESOLVEDThe dependency has been upgraded; the vulnerability is gone.The next SBOM upload will remove this finding automatically. This state confirms the fix was verified.
FALSE_POSITIVEThe vulnerability does not apply at all.Typically because the affected code path is never invoked, or the CVE was incorrectly assigned to this package version.

Analyst Comments

Free-text comments can be added to any finding. Comments should include:

  • The date and analyst name
  • The reason for the decision (e.g. mitigating control, unused code path)
  • Any linked tickets or evidence (e.g. JIRA issue, PR number)
  • A remediation timeline if applicable
Example analyst comment (CVE-2021-44228 / log4j-core 2.14.1)

Reviewed 2025-01-15 by J. Smith.
Our deployment uses the JNDI lookup feature disabled via log4j2.formatMsgNoLookups=true JVM flag set at container startup. Confirmed with DevOps — the attack vector is mitigated. Tracking upgrade to log4j 2.17.1 in Q1 sprint board (JIRA-4821).

Suppression

When a finding is accepted or not applicable, it can be suppressed so it no longer inflates your vulnerability counts or risk score.

  • Suppressed findings are hidden from the default dashboard view
  • They remain fully searchable with the Show suppressed filter
  • All original CVE details, CVSS scores, and analyst notes are preserved
  • Suppression can be revoked at any time — fully reversible
  • Suppressed findings are still included in the vulnerability PDF report
  • The audit trail records who suppressed a finding and when
⚠️  Important
Suppression is not remediation. Always pair suppression with a documented justification and a remediation timeline where applicable. Suppressed findings should be reviewed periodically to ensure the justification remains valid.

6. Automated Reporting

Jenkins pipelines generate shareable PDF reports from live Dependency-Track data. No manual export steps are required — reports are auto-archived as Jenkins build artefacts.

Security Analysis (Vulnerability) Report

  • One report per project, or across all active projects via exportAllDTReports()
  • Executive summary showing Critical / High / Medium / Low counts and overall risk level
  • Per-vulnerability detail: CVE ID, component, version, CVSS score
  • Analyst comments and suppression status included
  • Landscape A4 PDF — ready to share with management or auditors
  • Auto-archived as a Jenkins build artefact

SBOM Component Report

  • Full component inventory: name, version, supplier, licence
  • Supports single project or all active projects
  • Collection projects produce merged reports across all children
  • Useful for licence compliance and open-source governance audits
  • Flags components with missing or restrictive licences (GPL, UNKNOWN)
  • Landscape A4 PDF — consistent, branded format

7. Example Report Data

Sample — Security Analysis Report (customer-api v1.8.0)

The table below illustrates the per-finding detail included in the PDF vulnerability report:

CVE IDComponentVersionSeverityCVSSAnalysis StateDescription
CVE-2021-44228log4j-core2.14.1CRITICAL10.0NOT_AFFECTEDJNDI disabled via JVM flag — mitigated
CVE-2022-42003jackson-databind2.13.0HIGH7.5IN_TRIAGEResource consumption — under review
CVE-2023-20863spring-expression5.3.25HIGH6.5IN_TRIAGEReDoS — investigating impact
CVE-2022-45868h2database2.1.210HIGH7.8EXPLOITABLEUpgrade to 2.2.x scheduled sprint 14
CVE-2023-34042spring-security-web5.7.5MEDIUM5.3FALSE_POSITIVEUnused endpoint — confirmed FP
CVE-2023-20861spring-expression5.3.25MEDIUM5.9RESOLVEDUpgraded to 6.0.8 in PR #441

Sample — SBOM Component Report (my-billing-service v2.4.1)

The table below illustrates the component inventory included in the PDF SBOM report. 187 total components (43 direct, 144 transitive) across 12 unique licences:

ComponentVersionSupplierTypeLicenceScope
spring-boot-starter-web3.2.1Pivotal / VMwareLibraryApache-2.0Required
jackson-databind2.16.1FasterXMLLibraryApache-2.0Required
log4j-api2.22.1ApacheLibraryApache-2.0Required
hibernate-core6.4.2Red HatLibraryLGPL-2.1Required
commons-lang33.14.0ApacheLibraryApache-2.0Required
bouncy-castle-provider1.78Legion of the BCLibraryMITOptional
netty-handler4.1.107Netty ProjectLibraryApache-2.0Required
unknown-lib0.9.1(unknown)LibraryUNKNOWNRequired
📝  Licence colour key
Licence colours: Apache-2.0 MIT = permissive; LGPL-2.1 = weak copyleft (review required); UNKNOWN = action required.

8. Pipeline Overview

The end-to-end flow from source commit to vulnerability insight is fully automated via the Jenkins shared library.

  1. Developer pushes code to Git — Jenkins pipeline triggered
  2. SBOM generated via cdxgenRepo() or the CycloneDX Maven plugin
  3. uploadSBOM() posts the SBOM to Dependency-Track via REST API
  4. Dependency-Track enriches components against NVD, OSV, and GitHub Advisories
  5. Vulnerabilities are listed by severity with CVSS scores in the DT dashboard
  6. Team triages findings, records analysis, and suppresses accepted risks
  7. exportAllDTReports() generates PDF reports, archived as build artefacts

Jenkins Shared Library Reference

Shared Library FunctionPurposeTypical Usage
cdxgenRepo()Generate SBOM for any language repository using cdxgencdxgenRepo(env.SBOM_FILE, true, '', '17')
uploadSBOM()Upload SBOM and create/update Dependency-Track projectuploadSBOM(sbomFile: ..., projectName: ..., projectVersion: ...)
createDTCollectionProject()Group child projects into an aggregate collection with combined metricscreateDTCollectionProject(name: 'my-portfolio', version: '1.0')
exportDTReport()Generate vulnerability PDF for a single projectexportDTReport(projectName: ..., projectVersion: ...)
exportAllDTReports()Generate vulnerability PDFs for every active Dependency-Track projectexportAllDTReports(env.DEPENDENCY_TRACK_API_URL)
exportSBOMReport()Generate SBOM component PDF for a single projectexportSBOMReport(projectName: ..., projectVersion: ...)
grypeScan()Run Grype container image vulnerability scan as a pipeline stagegrypeScan(image: 'myapp:latest')

9. API Explorer — Swagger UI

Dependency-Track ships with a built-in Swagger / OpenAPI UI that gives you an interactive, browser-based explorer for every endpoint the platform exposes. It is the fastest way to understand what is possible and to prototype integrations before writing code.

ℹ️  Accessing the Swagger UI
Open http://<your-dt-host>/api/swagger-ui/ in a browser. Click Authorize in the top-right, enter your API key (from Administration → Access Management → API Keys), and every endpoint becomes executable directly from the browser.

What You Can Explore

Endpoint GroupPath PrefixKey Operations
BOM / SBOM Upload/api/v1/bomUpload a CycloneDX or SPDX BOM; poll processing status
Projects/api/v1/projectCreate, update, delete, tag, and search projects; manage versions and parent/child relationships
Findings / Vulnerabilities/api/v1/findingList all findings for a project; export as FPF or SARIF; summary counts by severity
Analysis/api/v1/analysisRead and write analysis state, justification, comments, suppression — programmatic triage
Vulnerability/api/v1/vulnerabilityLook up a specific CVE; list all vulns for a component; source feed status
Component/api/v1/componentSearch components across the portfolio; resolve package URLs; identify all projects affected by a component
Policies/api/v1/policyCreate and manage licence and severity policies; list violations
Notifications/api/v1/notificationConfigure Slack, e-mail, webhook, and MS Teams alert subscriptions
Metrics/api/v1/metricsPortfolio-level and per-project risk scores; trend data over time

Practical Uses of the Swagger UI

  • Prototype automations — try an endpoint interactively before writing a Jenkins step or script
  • Debug uploads — POST a BOM directly and inspect the raw JSON response
  • Bulk triage — use the PUT /api/v1/analysis endpoint to set analysis state across many findings from a script
  • Build dashboards — GET /api/v1/metrics/portfolio returns live counts suitable for Grafana or a custom status page
  • Impact analysis — GET /api/v1/component/identity with a package URL instantly shows every project that includes a component
  • Policy automation — create licence policies via POST /api/v1/policy and wire them to break-build notifications
💡  OpenAPI Spec in this Repository
The full OpenAPI specification (dependencytrack-openapi.yaml) is also available in this repository. Import it into Postman, Insomnia, or any REST client for a complete offline API reference with saved example requests.
📝  Everything in the UI is in the API
All pipeline operations in this project — SBOM upload, project creation, findings export, analysis recording — use the same REST API the Swagger UI exposes. If something is in the UI it can also be automated.

Integration Ecosystem

The Dependency-Track REST API enables integration with ticketing systems (Jira), SIEMs (Splunk, Elasticsearch), and executive dashboards — giving full visibility from developer laptop to boardroom. Any tool that can make an HTTP request can integrate with Dependency-Track.

IntegrationAPI Endpoint(s) UsedWhat It Enables
Jira / TicketingGET /api/v1/finding/{project}
POST /api/v1/analysis		Auto-create Jira issues for Critical/High findings; sync analysis state back when tickets are resolved
Splunk / ElasticsearchGET /api/v1/finding/{project}
Notification webhooks		Stream vulnerability events and analysis decisions into your SIEM for correlation, alerting, and compliance reporting
Grafana DashboardsGET /api/v1/metrics/portfolio
GET /api/v1/metrics/project/{uuid}		Pull live risk scores and trend data into Grafana panels for real-time executive-level visibility
MS Teams / SlackPOST /api/v1/notification/publisherConfigure webhook notifications for new Critical or High findings so the right team is alerted immediately
PagerDuty / OpsGenieOutbound webhook from notification rulesTrigger on-call alerts when newly introduced Critical vulnerabilities are detected in production-bound builds
Custom / Executive DashboardsGET /api/v1/metrics/portfolio
GET /api/v1/project		Schedule daily metrics exports; feed bespoke reporting tools or management dashboards with live risk data
ℹ️  Built-in Notification Channels
Dependency-Track also supports outbound notifications via Slack, MS Teams, e-mail, and generic webhooks — all configurable from Administration → Notifications. Each notification can be scoped to specific projects, portfolios, or severity levels so the right team gets exactly the signal they need.

10. Benefits Summary

Adopting the SBOM pipeline and Dependency-Track gives engineering, security, and compliance teams a single, continuous view of software risk — with no manual overhead.

🔎

Complete Visibility

Every dependency — direct and transitive — is inventoried. No blind spots in your software supply chain.

💡

Continuous Monitoring

New CVEs are automatically matched against your component inventory. Updates arrive as soon as vulnerability feeds refresh — no manual scans.

✅

Structured Triage

Analysis states, analyst comments, and suppression give every security decision a governed, auditable home inside the platform.

📄

Automated Reporting

Shareable PDF reports (vulnerability & SBOM) generated automatically on every build. No manual export effort required.

🚀

Shift-Left Security

Maven builds upload SBOMs at build time — vulnerabilities surface during development, not after production deployment.

🛡

Policy Enforcement

Define severity thresholds and licence rules. Violations break the build before bad dependencies ever reach a release artefact.

🌐

Polyglot Coverage

Maven, Node.js, Python, Go, Docker images — cdxgen handles all ecosystems with a single Jenkins shared-library step.

🔧

REST API & Integrations

Full headless API enables Jira, Splunk, Grafana, and custom tooling integrations. Every UI action is automatable.

👤

Audit-Ready

Every analysis state change, comment, and suppression is timestamped and attributed. Compliance evidence is generated as a byproduct of normal work.

💡  Integration Ecosystem
The Dependency-Track REST API enables integration with ticketing systems (Jira), SIEMs (Splunk, Elasticsearch), and executive dashboards — giving full visibility from developer laptop to boardroom.
